questions.text: I think that analytical composure during distressing events allows me to act in ways that are both effective and fair.
I experience joy as a deep, settled feeling rather than an effervescent high.
I recover quickly from emotional events and return to my baseline without much effort.
I believe that building systems and routines is the smartest investment of time.
I prefer when someone else organizes the agenda and I can support their plan.
I believe that the moral significance of suffering is absolute — it does not depend on context, timing, or the identity of the person involved.
I prefer to help execute a shared vision rather than set one alone.
I rarely notice when someone is looking to me for a social cue.
I believe that strong personal conviction is necessary for effective leadership.
I take pride in my ability to think on my feet and handle the unexpected.
I find that focusing on my own path keeps me grounded and purposeful.
I pick up on the way people rearrange their expressions when they realize they are being observed.
I hold the view that people should ensure their own stability before worrying about others.
I believe that the depth of my emotional experience gives life its meaning, even when it's painful.
I find that deflecting praise and redirecting it to others feels natural and right.
I believe commitments should be flexible enough to accommodate new information.
I tend to be surprised when someone tells me about interpersonal tension I had not noticed.
I am more inclined to formulate a plan of action than to dwell on the emotional dimensions of a difficult situation.
